summary: Gruber argues that Malcolm Gladwell's New Yorker piece, which characterized Steve Jobs as a 'tweaker' rather than a visionary, is fundamentally flawed — and that the flaw traces back to Walter Isaacson's biography. Gruber contends that Jobs defies simple categorization as either inventor or tweaker, and that reducing the Mac, iPod, iPhone, and iPad to mere iterations of prior technology misses the radical nature of what Apple actually accomplished. He demonstrates this by showing how the Macintosh democratized computing from a $100,000 workstation to a $2,500 personal computer, and how pre-iPhone smartphones bear no resemblance to what followed. Gruber argues that if anyone fits the 'tweaker' label, it's Bill Gates — though even that is unfair to Gates's genuine vision around software as a business. The core problem is that Gladwell's either/or framework of visionary versus tweaker cannot capture what Jobs actually did.
key_points: Isaacson's biography failed to explore the mystery of what Jobs's actual gifts were, leading downstream commentators like Gladwell astray
The differences between pre-Apple products and Apple's versions weren't 'tweaks' — the Mac brought $100K workstation concepts to a $2,500 mass-market computer, which is radical innovation
Gladwell's claim that the iPad idea 'came from a Microsoft engineer' confuses motivation with invention
If the tweaker label fits anyone in tech, it's Bill Gates — though Gates was a genuine visionary about software's value, just never a product visionary
Jobs was both visionary and tweaker: he re-imagined the world and then actually made it happen through relentless iteration
money_quotes: Jobs understood technology but was not an engineer. He had profoundly exquisite taste but was not a designer. What it was that Jobs actually did is much of the mystery of his life and his work.
If this is the standard for innovation, then what product, from any company, has truly been innovative?
Bringing the concepts of a $100,000 networked workstation to a $2500 standalone mass market personal computer is, I say, radically innovative.
A 'large-scale visionary' is precisely what Steve Jobs was.
If anyone is the 'tweaker' in the PC industry, clearly it's Bill Gates, not Steve Jobs.
Gates was (and remains) a large-scale visionary in his own right. He was simply never a product visionary.
The man pioneered the concept of selling software.
Steve Jobs really did re-imagine the world. The thing is, he actually made it happen, too.
key_insight: Gladwell's visionary-versus-tweaker framework fundamentally mischaracterizes Jobs because it cannot account for someone who both reimagined the world and then relentlessly iterated to make that vision real.
